scenario: FEEL needs inline function definition -- define and use in one expression. Wonders syntax.
question: FEEL inline function definition?

options.text: While BKM nodes are the formal DMN mechanism for reusable decision logic, FEEL also supports inline function definitions directly within expressions using the function(params) expression syntax.

md:30-45: the function(params) expression syntax allows defining anonymous functions directly within a FEEL expression without requiring a BKM node. These functions can be stored in context entries and invoked by name within the same expression scope. This enables functional-style programming patterns in FEEL, including higher-order functions where built-in functions like sort or filter accept function arguments. Inline functions are particularly useful for encapsulating complex logic within a single decision or expression without DMN structural overhead.

FEEL function definitions use Java-style method signatures with return type declarations and access modifiers -- this does not accurately reflect the behavior. FEEL is a functional expression language distinct from Java. Function definitions use the form function(param1, param2) body-expression with no type annotations, access modifiers, or return type declarations. Parameters are positional and types are inferred from usage. There are no classes, methods, or Java-style constructs in FEEL. The language is designed to be readable by business analysts, not Java developers.

a.text: Incorrect. FEEL inline functions: Syntax (DMN FEEL spec): `feel function(parameters) returnExpression Examples: feel function(x) x 2 function(a, b) a + b function(name: string) "Hello, " + name Usage: Bind via context literal (Camunda FEEL has NO let..in syntax -- context literal is the canonical binding form per feel-context-expressions.md:35-43): feel { double: function(x) x 2, result: double(5) } // {double:..., result:10} Doc example verbatim from feel-context-expressions.md:177-183: feel { age: function(birthday) (today() - birthday).years, ages: for birthday in birthdays return age(birthday), minAge: min(ages) }.minAge Pass to higher-order functions: feel sort([3, 1, 2], function(a, b) a Literal expression (per dmn.md:66-74) -- wrap the FEEL function inside the literal expression body, give the BKM name (used as result variable), add parameters via the () UI. For BKM (per Set 14 Q25): BKM contains function. Reusable across decisions. Stronger than inline: for reuse. Inline vs BKM: | Aspect | Inline | BKM | |--------|--------|-----| | Scope | Single expression | Reusable across decisions | | Visibility | Local | DMN-model-wide | | Use case | One-off | Reusable logic | For Camunda 8 FEEL support (confirmed): Inline function syntax per feel-functions.md:30-45: function(args) body. Higher-order function support: sort(list, function(a,b) a b.priority) Sort by priority descending. Use case 3: Filtering (no function(...) wrapper needed -- direct predicate is the canonical FEEL filter form per feel-context-expressions.md:88-113): feel items[amount > 100] The loop variable item is implicit; element entries are accessed by key directly. Doc verbatim cite at :98-99 "While filtering, the current element is assigned to the variable item and its entries can be accessed by their key." For DMN-only logic: No Boxed Function in C8 Modeler per dmn.md:23-28. Use BKM with literal expression (per dmn.md:66-74) -- give the BKM a name (= result var), add () params, write function(x) body inside the literal expression cell. For migration / refactoring: Inline function used multiple times: extract to BKM. BKM used once: maybe inline. For tests: Test functions inline: in unit test contexts. Verify behavior. For complex callbacks: External worker more flexible: Java / JS. FEEL functions: simple cases. Anti-patterns: Overly complex inline functions: hard to read. Inline when BKM fits: reuse forgone. Best practices: Use for simple one-off logic. Extract to BKM for reuse. Type parameters for clarity. Test functions. For the scenario* (inline function): feel function(x) x 2 `` Standard FEEL syntax (verify per Camunda 8). For verify: Camunda Modeler: try syntax.
